allusions.suspected_source: Plato, Phaedrus 230b (the plane-tree by the Ilissus)
allusions.translator_note: Scaevola explicitly invokes the opening scene of Plato's Phaedrus to set the dialogue under the plane-tree at Tusculum.
Crassus's reading of the Gorgias at Athens with Charmadas; Plato deriding orators while himself the supreme orator.

allusions.surface: Ithacam illam in asperrimis saxulis tamquam nidulum adfixam
allusions.translator_note: Odysseus's praise of his rugged Ithaca; the figure of the wisest man preferring his rocky homeland to immortality.

allusions.surface: egregie cordatus homo, catus Aelius Sextus
allusions.translator_note: Cited as the supreme poet's tribute to the jurisconsult Sex. Aelius Paetus.
Crassus quotes Apollo's words about giving counsel to the uncertain — 'quos ego ope mea ex incertis certos compotesque consili dimitto'. Place uncertain.

allusions.suspected_source: XII Tables, Tabula 6 (the legal formula for verbal disposition)
allusions.translator_note: The famous Tabula 6 phrase on inheritance-by-tongue; Antonius cites it ironically against Crassus's exaltation of the XII Tables.

allusions.surface: segregare abs te ausu's ausus es
allusions.translator_note: From a lost Roman tragedy on Telamon, with Telamon raging at his son Ajax. Cited by Antonius as a model of the actor's burning eyes through the mask.

allusions.surface: cum aetate exacta indigem liberum lacerasti
allusions.suspected_source: Pacuvius, Niptra (continuation of the same tragedy)
allusions.translator_note: The piteous note of Telamon mourning, cited in the same passage.
Catulus quotes Plautus's 'non possum quin exclamem' to introduce his own outburst of praise.

allusions.surface: ut Plautum mihi aut Naevium videar audire
allusions.translator_note: Laelia's archaic Roman speech evokes the early Roman comic and epic dramatist.

allusions.surface: ille enim turpe sibi ait esse tacere
allusions.translator_note: Aristotle's saying: 'It is base to be silent and let Isocrates speak' — adapted from a verse of Euripides's Philoctetes.
A long passage of high tragic storm-imagery, used to illustrate metaphor.
Ulysses's dying words in Pacuvius's Niptra; cited for the apt metaphor 'rape' (snatch up).

allusions.surface: Quo me miser conferam? Quo vertam?
allusions.suspected_source: Gaius Gracchus, public speech (after Tiberius's death)
allusions.translator_note: C. Gracchus's most famous surviving line, on the choice of refuges after his brother's murder. Crassus quotes it to illustrate Gracchus's actio.

From the Thyestean tragedy. Cicero gives a series of voice-colour examples from Accius's Atreus and Pacuvius's tragedies — the most extensive surviving sample of how Roman tragic verses were 'coloured' in delivery.
